title: Chapter 3 — Play across several screens
hero.title: Play across several screens
hero.lead: The TV shows the match; the phones act as controllers. So everyone sees the same thing at the same time, we need a referee: the server.
s1.lead: Until now, everything happened on a single computer. Now: a TV, two phones… three different devices that must live the same match, perfectly in sync.
s1.p1: If each device computed the match in its own corner, they'd end up disagreeing (a ball here, not there…). We need a single source of truth.
s2.p1: We add a program running “in the middle”: the server. The devices are clients. The server is the only one to compute the match (the physics, the scores); the clients only display what it decides and send it the players' orders.
s2.li1: 📱 The phones send: “my angle = 60°, I confirm”.
s2.li2: 🧑‍⚖️ The server computes the volley, who gets hit, the score.
s2.li3: 📺 Everyone receives the game state and displays it the same way.
s2.callout: Remember. “The referee is always right”: since the server decides everything, the screens can't contradict each other — and cheating is harder.
s3.h2: A phone line that stays open: the WebSocket
s3.p1: A classic web page asks for a piece of info, gets an answer, then hangs up. Too slow for a game! We use a WebSocket: a line that stays open both ways. The server can “speak” whenever it wants, ~30 times per second.
s3.callout.try: Try it! Click to launch a volley and watch the messages travel: the orders go up to the referee, then the state comes back down to every screen.
s3.sim.title: The dance of the messages
s3.sim.hint: Blue/red: the players' orders. Green: the game state sent back to everyone.
s4.h2: The same landscape everywhere, without sending it all
s4.p1: The terrain can be thousands of points: too big to send. The trick: the server only sends a small random seed (one number). Each screen recomputes the same landscape from that seed (remember the generator from chapter 2!).
s4.code.cap: the idea of sharing by seed
s5.h2: Joining a match: room code & QR
s5.p1: The TV creates a room and shows a 4-letter code and a QR code. The phones scan the QR: they land straight into the right match. Handy!
s5.fig.cap: The TV shows the code & the QR; two phones join as players.
s5.callout.nerd: For the curious. The QR must point to the TV's local network address (something like 192.168.1.20), not to “localhost” (which means “myself”). Otherwise the phone would be looking for itself! The server finds the right address on its own.
s6.h2: Who does what? The TV manages on its own
s6.li1: 📺 A detected TV starts straight away in “host” mode: no remote needed.
s6.li2: 🟢 The match starts on its own as soon as the 2nd player has joined.
s6.li3: 🧑‍🤝‍🧑 Extra players? They watch while waiting for a spot to free up, then take over.
s6.callout: Remember. Batch 3 = going from a game “on one screen” to a “networked” game, without rewriting everything: we just moved the computation to the referee.
